basic_info.name: Phala Network (TEE Agent Compute)
basic_info.status: Production (Phala Network mainnet live since 2021; Phala Cloud launched January 2025 for TEE-based agent/dApp hosting; 30,000+ TEE worker devices in the network; Decentralized Root of Trust (DeRoT) and Key Management Protocol introduced 2024-2025; Phat Contracts deprecated in favor of Phala Cloud; active partnerships with AI agent ecosystem including AI Agent Contract framework)
basic_info.ecosystem: Polkadot (Phala is a Polkadot parachain via Substrate; PHA token on Polkadot/Ethereum via bridges); Phala Cloud supports deployment of agents that interact with Ethereum, Base, Arbitrum, and other EVM chains; cross-chain bridges to Ethereum (Khala on Kusama as canary network); expanding multi-chain agent support
basic_info.launch_date: 2021 (Phala mainnet and Khala canary network launched; TEE mining network operational since 2021; Phala Cloud launched January 2025; DeRoT and Key Management Protocol announced late 2024/early 2025; Phat Contracts (off-chain compute) launched 2023) [uncertain]

onboarding_flow.registration_steps: 1) Developer creates an account on Phala Cloud (cloud.phala.network) via web interface -- supports GitHub OAuth, wallet connection, or email registration. 2) Select deployment type: TEE-secured Docker container for agent deployment. Phala Cloud provides pre-configured templates for AI agents (e.g., AI Agent Contract templates, LLM hosting, autonomous agent frameworks). 3) Upload or configure the agent code/container image. The agent code runs inside an Intel SGX or Intel TDX Trusted Execution Environment on Phala workers. 4) Phala Cloud assigns the workload to a TEE worker in the decentralized network. The worker generates a TEE attestation report proving the code is running in a genuine enclave. 5) DeRoT (Decentralized Root of Trust) verification: the attestation is verified through Phala's decentralized attestation infrastructure rather than relying solely on Intel's centralized attestation service (IAS/DCAP). Multiple attestation verifiers cross-check the TEE report. 6) Key Management Protocol: the agent receives cryptographic keys generated and managed within TEE enclaves. Keys are derived through Phala's decentralized key management system where key shares are distributed across multiple TEE workers, preventing any single worker from accessing the full key. 7) The agent is assigned a unique identifier and endpoint on Phala Cloud. 8) For on-chain identity: the agent can register on Phala's parachain with an on-chain record linking to its TEE attestation proof and deployment metadata. 9) The agent is now operational within the TEE enclave, with network connectivity for interacting with blockchains, APIs, and other agents. 10) For AI Agent Contract deployments: the framework provides built-in tooling for autonomous agent behaviors including signing transactions, reading on-chain state, and interacting with off-chain data sources -- all within the TEE boundary.
onboarding_flow.human_involvement: full-setup (human developer must register on Phala Cloud, configure the agent deployment, upload container images, and initiate deployment; once deployed, the agent operates autonomously within its TEE enclave; Phala Cloud dashboard provides monitoring and management UI; agent runtime operations require no ongoing human intervention)
onboarding_flow.agent_autonomy: High autonomy after deployment. Once deployed in a TEE enclave, the agent operates fully autonomously: it can sign transactions using TEE-managed keys, interact with blockchains, call external APIs, communicate with other agents, and process data -- all within the secure enclave. The agent cannot self-register on Phala Cloud (human must initiate deployment). However, the AI Agent Contract framework supports autonomous agent behaviors including multi-step reasoning, tool use, and blockchain interactions without human approval. Key operations (key generation, attestation refresh, computation) happen automatically within the TEE.
onboarding_flow.time_to_first_action: minutes (Phala Cloud deployment typically takes minutes for container setup and TEE initialization; TEE attestation generation takes seconds; DeRoT verification adds seconds to minutes; once the enclave is running, the agent can act immediately)

identity_model.identity_type: TEE attestation-based identity + wallet. Agent identity is primarily established through TEE remote attestation -- a cryptographic proof that the agent code is running in a genuine, unmodified enclave on verified hardware. The attestation report includes: hardware identity (CPUSVN, platform measurements), enclave measurement (MRENCLAVE -- hash of the code running inside), signer identity (MRSIGNER), and configuration details. Additionally, agents can have on-chain wallet addresses for blockchain interactions. The DeRoT system provides a decentralized identity anchor beyond Intel's centralized attestation.
identity_model.key_management: Multi-layered TEE-based key management via Phala's Key Management Protocol: (1) Enclave-generated keys: each TEE enclave generates its own key pair during initialization. The private key never leaves the enclave. (2) Key Management Protocol: for persistent keys that survive enclave restarts, Phala uses a distributed key management system where key shares are stored across multiple TEE workers. A threshold of workers must collaborate to reconstruct or use the key. (3) DeRoT key hierarchy: master keys are protected by the Decentralized Root of Trust, with derived keys for specific agent operations. (4) Key rotation: new enclave instantiation generates new ephemeral keys; persistent keys can be rotated through the Key Management Protocol by re-sharing across the TEE worker set. (5) Key revocation: compromised worker nodes are removed from the key management cluster, and keys re-shared among remaining honest workers. (6) Hardware sealing: Intel SGX sealing keys allow encrypted key storage on local disk, recoverable only by the same enclave on the same hardware. (7) All key operations happen within TEE boundaries -- no plaintext key material is exposed to the host OS or Phala operators.
identity_model.multi_instance_support: Yes. Multiple TEE workers can run instances of the same agent code (matching MRENCLAVE). The Key Management Protocol enables key sharing across instances, allowing multiple enclaves to operate under the same identity. This supports high-availability deployments and horizontal scaling of agent compute. Each instance independently generates TEE attestations but can share persistent identity keys through the distributed key management system. [uncertain]
identity_model.recovery_mechanism: TEE-native recovery through the Key Management Protocol. If a single TEE worker fails, the distributed key shares held by other workers allow key reconstruction in a new enclave. The threshold scheme (e.g., k-of-n) ensures that loss of individual workers does not result in key loss as long as the threshold of workers remains available. For catastrophic scenarios (network-wide TEE failure), recovery depends on sealed key backups and the hardware sealing mechanism. Phala Cloud likely provides backup and migration tooling. If the deployer's Phala Cloud account credentials are lost, standard account recovery (email/OAuth) applies. [uncertain]
identity_model.versioning_support: Implicit via TEE measurements. Every agent deployment has a unique MRENCLAVE value (hash of the enclave code). Different versions of the same agent produce different MRENCLAVE values, providing cryptographic version identification. Phala Cloud tracks deployment history and container image versions. On-chain, attestation reports can be stored or referenced, creating a verifiable version trail. The AI Agent Contract framework supports versioned deployments. No formal on-chain version append mechanism like Olas, but the TEE measurement system inherently provides version integrity. [uncertain]

security.authentication_method: TEE remote attestation (Intel SGX EPID/DCAP attestation verified through DeRoT -- decentralized verification rather than Intel IAS alone; attestation proves hardware genuineness, code integrity, and enclave configuration); wallet signature (for on-chain transactions using keys managed within TEE); challenge-response (Phala Cloud API authentication for management operations)
security.known_vulnerabilities: 1) TEE side-channel attacks: Intel SGX has known vulnerability classes including Spectre/Meltdown variants, Foreshadow (L1TF), Plundervolt, SGAxe, and other microarchitectural attacks. While mitigations exist (microcode updates, software patches), the attack surface is inherent to SGX architecture. Phala's use of Intel TDX for newer deployments addresses some SGX-specific vulnerabilities. 2) Intel SGX deprecation risk: Intel has been gradually deprecating SGX support on consumer CPUs (removed from 12th-gen+), though server-grade Xeon processors retain SGX support. This affects the long-term worker supply. 3) Centralization of TEE hardware: ultimate trust anchor is Intel's hardware and attestation infrastructure; DeRoT mitigates the attestation centralization but not the hardware trust root. 4) Worker collusion: if a threshold of TEE workers running the Key Management Protocol are compromised (or if Intel's root of trust is broken), key material could be exposed. 5) Side-channel through enclave I/O: even with TEE protection, the host OS can observe memory access patterns, network traffic patterns, and timing -- potentially leaking information about agent operations. 6) Supply chain attacks on TEE hardware are a theoretical concern. 7) Phala workers are permissionless -- any TEE device can join, raising concerns about adversarial workers running modified firmware. DeRoT attestation is designed to catch this. [uncertain]
security.revocation_speed: minutes (worker removal from the TEE cluster can be initiated via on-chain governance or Phala Cloud management; enclave termination is immediate once the host OS kills the enclave process; key revocation through the Key Management Protocol requires re-sharing across remaining workers, taking minutes; Phala Cloud API-level access revocation is near-instant) [uncertain]
security.anti_sybil: staking (PHA token staking required for TEE workers to join the network -- workers must stake PHA to participate in compute tasks, creating economic barrier for malicious workers); TEE attestation (hardware attestation proves genuine TEE hardware, preventing virtual/emulated workers); fee (Phala Cloud charges for compute resources, preventing free mass agent creation); hardware requirement (physical TEE-capable hardware is required to operate a worker node, creating a significant capital barrier to Sybil attacks at the worker level)
security.data_exposure_risk: Minimal by design -- TEE enclaves protect all in-memory data from the host OS and network observers. Agent code, keys, and runtime state are encrypted within the enclave. TEE attestation reports are public and reveal MRENCLAVE (code hash) and hardware metadata, which could reveal what agent software is running. Phala Cloud deployment metadata (container images, configurations) may be visible to the platform. On-chain transactions signed by the agent are publicly visible. Network traffic patterns (timing, size, destination) are observable by the host even though content is encrypted. Phala Cloud account information follows standard platform data handling.
security.bot_to_bot_attack_surface: TEE provides strong isolation for agent computation -- malicious inter-agent messages cannot directly compromise the enclave's execution environment. However: (1) Agent application logic is not inherently protected against prompt injection or semantic attacks at the input processing layer. (2) The TEE protects the execution environment but does not validate the semantic correctness of inputs. (3) If an agent accepts and processes untrusted inputs from other agents (common in multi-agent systems), application-level vulnerabilities apply regardless of TEE protection. (4) Network-level attacks (DDoS, man-in-the-middle for non-TLS traffic) can disrupt agent communication. (5) Phala does not provide built-in agent-to-agent communication protocols with semantic validation -- this is application-layer responsibility. [uncertain]
security.skill_plugin_vetting: Limited built-in vetting. Phala Cloud deploys user-provided Docker containers in TEE enclaves -- the platform does not perform automated security scanning or code auditing of deployed agent code. The TEE attestation (MRENCLAVE) provides cryptographic proof of what code is running, enabling external verification but not automatic vetting. The AI Agent Contract framework provides some structured tooling for agent behaviors, but plugin/skill sandboxing within the enclave is not a core platform feature. Users are responsible for the security of their deployed code. Community-published templates on Phala Cloud may undergo informal review. [uncertain]
security.attestation_mechanism: TEE remote attestation via DeRoT (Decentralized Root of Trust): (1) Intel SGX EPID or DCAP attestation generates a hardware-signed report proving enclave integrity (MRENCLAVE, MRSIGNER, hardware version). (2) DeRoT decentralizes the attestation verification -- instead of relying solely on Intel Attestation Service (IAS), multiple independent verifiers in Phala's network cross-check attestation reports. This creates a decentralized trust anchor resistant to single-point-of-failure in Intel's infrastructure. (3) Attestation reports can be verified on-chain, enabling smart contracts to confirm that an agent is running in a genuine TEE. (4) Continuous attestation: workers periodically re-attest to prove ongoing integrity, not just at initialization. (5) The Key Management Protocol integrates with attestation -- key shares are only distributed to workers with valid attestation.

offchain_onchain_linking.linking_support: yes (native -- Phala is a blockchain-TEE hybrid where on-chain state and off-chain TEE computation are tightly integrated; attestation reports bridge on-chain verifiable identity with off-chain secure computation)
offchain_onchain_linking.linking_mechanism: TEE attestation anchored on-chain: Phala workers submit TEE attestation reports to the Phala parachain, creating an on-chain record that links the worker's blockchain identity (staking address) to its TEE hardware identity and the specific code running in the enclave (MRENCLAVE). Smart contracts on Phala or other chains can verify these attestation records to confirm that a specific agent is running in a genuine TEE. The Key Management Protocol bridges on-chain governance (which workers are authorized) with off-chain key material (distributed within TEEs). Phala Cloud provides API endpoints (off-chain) linked to on-chain worker/staking records. Cross-chain: agents running in Phala TEEs can sign transactions for any blockchain, linking the TEE-based identity to on-chain actions on Ethereum, Polkadot, and other networks.
offchain_onchain_linking.transfer_behavior: Agent deployments on Phala Cloud are tied to the deployer's account, not to a transferable token. If the underlying TEE worker changes (due to migration or failure), the Key Management Protocol enables key continuity -- the agent's identity can persist across hardware changes. On-chain staking positions (PHA) can be transferred. The Phala parachain does not use an ERC-721-style agent identity token, so there is no direct NFT transfer behavior. Migration between workers is handled by the Key Management Protocol rather than token transfer. [uncertain]

delegation_and_authorization.delegation_model: TEE-mediated delegation: (1) The human deployer delegates operational authority to the agent by deploying it in a TEE enclave and configuring its permitted behaviors (via code/configuration). The TEE enforces that the agent operates only within its programmed logic. (2) The Key Management Protocol provides key-level delegation -- specific keys can be derived for specific operations, allowing fine-grained control over what the agent can sign. (3) For on-chain operations: the agent's TEE-managed wallet can be authorized via standard smart contract mechanisms (approve, allowance). (4) No formal OAuth OBO, OIDC-A, or mandate-based delegation protocol built into Phala. Delegation is primarily through code-level constraints enforced by the TEE enclave.
delegation_and_authorization.authorization_granularity: Fine-grained via code-level enforcement in TEE. The TEE enclave enforces whatever authorization logic is coded into the agent -- this can range from simple role checks to complex ABAC policies, spending limits, or whitelist-based controls. The Key Management Protocol can issue purpose-specific derived keys. However, this granularity is application-defined, not protocol-defined -- Phala provides the secure execution environment, and the agent developer implements the authorization logic within it.

discovery_and_interoperability.discovery_mechanism: Phala Cloud dashboard and API (agents deployed on Phala Cloud can be discovered through the platform's interface and API endpoints); on-chain registry (workers and their attestation status can be queried from the Phala parachain); no built-in agent discovery registry in the A2A/Agent Card sense -- Phala focuses on secure compute infrastructure rather than agent discovery. Third-party agent registries (ERC-8004, Olas, etc.) could reference agents running on Phala. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.capability_declaration: No native capability declaration mechanism at the protocol level. Phala provides TEE compute infrastructure, not an agent discovery framework. Agents deployed on Phala Cloud have deployment metadata (container description, endpoints) but no structured capability advertisement (no Agent Card, JSON-LD, or manifest standard). The MRENCLAVE hash identifies what code is running but does not describe capabilities semantically. Application-layer capability declaration would need to be built by the agent developer. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.cross_platform_portability: Partially portable. Agent code in Docker containers is portable across any TEE-capable infrastructure (not locked to Phala specifically). TEE attestation is hardware-specific (Intel SGX/TDX) but the attestation verification via DeRoT can be checked from any chain. Keys managed through the Key Management Protocol are tied to Phala's TEE worker network. The Polkadot/Substrate-based identity is specific to Phala's parachain. Agents can interact with multiple blockchains via their TEE-managed keys, enabling operational cross-chain reach. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.standards_compliance: Intel SGX/TDX (TEE attestation standards -- EPID, DCAP); Substrate/Polkadot (parachain standards); Docker/OCI (container runtime for agent deployment); No explicit W3C DID, W3C VC, OIDC, SPIFFE, OAuth 2.0, ERC-721, ERC-8004, A2A, or MCP compliance. The DeRoT and Key Management Protocol are Phala-specific standards. Potential future alignment with emerging TEE attestation standards. [uncertain]
discovery_and_interoperability.protocol_composability: Moderate. Phala TEEs can run any agent framework/protocol inside the enclave, making it composable as an infrastructure layer: (1) Can run MCP servers inside TEE for secure tool hosting, (2) Can run A2A-compatible agents with TEE-backed attestation, (3) Can integrate with ERC-8004 by providing TEE-attested agent identities, (4) Can serve as a secure signing backend for Lit Protocol, Autonolas, or other agent platforms, (5) AI Agent Contract framework integrates with popular LLM providers and blockchain toolkits. Phala positions itself as the 'confidential compute layer' for any agent protocol. [uncertain]

push_pull_communication.push_support: WebSocket / HTTPS (Phala Cloud agents can expose HTTP/WebSocket endpoints for push communication; on-chain events on the Phala parachain emit events for worker registration, attestation updates, and staking changes; no built-in agent-specific push notification protocol) [uncertain]
push_pull_communication.pull_support: REST API (Phala Cloud provides API for querying deployment status, agent endpoints, and metrics); on-chain reads (Phala parachain state queries for worker attestation, staking, and governance data via Substrate RPC); agents expose their own HTTP endpoints for pull-based queries
push_pull_communication.subscription_model: On-chain events (Substrate-based event subscriptions on the Phala parachain for attestation, staking, and governance events); Phala Cloud may provide webhook or notification features for deployment lifecycle events; no built-in agent-to-agent subscription protocol -- application-layer implementation required. [uncertain]

trust_and_reputation.trust_model: Decentralized (hardware-based trust via TEE attestation + economic trust via staking). Trust is established through: (1) TEE hardware attestation -- cryptographic proof of genuine enclave execution verified via DeRoT, (2) PHA token staking by workers -- economic commitment and slashing risk, (3) Decentralized Root of Trust -- removes single-point-of-failure in Intel's attestation, (4) Continuous re-attestation ensures ongoing integrity, (5) Transparent worker performance metrics on-chain. The trust model is uniquely hardware-rooted compared to purely economic (staking) or reputation-based systems.
trust_and_reputation.reputation_system: Implicit, worker-level. Phala tracks worker performance metrics including uptime, task completion rate, and attestation validity. Workers with poor performance may lose staking rewards or be penalized. No formal agent-level reputation registry (like ERC-8004). The TEE attestation itself serves as a binary trust signal -- either the enclave is genuine or it is not. There is no nuanced reputation scoring for agents deployed on Phala Cloud. External reputation systems would need to be layered on top. [uncertain]

payment_and_economics.payment_integration: Native token (PHA token for staking, compute payments, and network governance); Phala Cloud likely supports fiat payment for compute resources (standard cloud billing); agents within TEE enclaves can integrate with any payment protocol by signing transactions for any supported blockchain -- AP2, x402, or custom payment flows can be implemented at the application layer within the TEE.
payment_and_economics.economic_model: Staking + compute fees. (1) Workers must stake PHA tokens to join the network and earn compute rewards. (2) Phala Cloud charges for compute resources (CPU/memory/storage within TEE enclaves) -- pricing model is usage-based. (3) PHA token economics: workers earn PHA for providing TEE compute; the tokenomics include inflation for compute rewards and burning mechanisms. (4) No separate onboarding fee for agents -- costs are compute-resource-based. (5) DeRoT and Key Management Protocol operations are part of the network's operational costs covered by staking economics. [uncertain]

governance_and_compliance.regulatory_alignment: Potential alignment with privacy/data protection regulations. TEE-based computation provides strong data confidentiality guarantees that may support GDPR compliance (data processed within encrypted enclaves, never exposed to operators). No explicit EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, or ISO 42001 compliance documented. The privacy-preserving nature of TEE computation is a selling point for regulated industries. Phala has positioned its technology for enterprise privacy requirements. [uncertain]
governance_and_compliance.audit_trail_capability: Blockchain anchoring + TEE attestation chain. (1) Worker attestation reports are stored/verifiable on the Phala parachain, creating an immutable record of TEE integrity over time. (2) Agent transactions signed by TEE-managed keys are recorded on their respective blockchains. (3) The Phala parachain records staking events, worker registration, and attestation updates. (4) Within the TEE enclave, agents can maintain local audit logs protected by enclave encryption. (5) MRENCLAVE values provide cryptographic proof of which code version produced which outputs. Cross-referencing on-chain attestation with on-chain agent actions creates a verifiable audit trail linking agent behavior to verified code.
governance_and_compliance.lifecycle_management: Registration (deploy agent container on Phala Cloud, assigned to TEE worker) / activation (TEE attestation verified via DeRoT, keys provisioned via Key Management Protocol, agent endpoints live) / suspension (Phala Cloud provides deployment pause/stop functionality; on-chain staking slash or worker removal disables compute) / migration (Key Management Protocol enables agent migration to new TEE workers while preserving keys and identity) / upgrade (deploy new container version with updated MRENCLAVE; Key Management Protocol can provision keys to the new enclave) / decommissioning (terminate deployment on Phala Cloud; enclave destroyed, ephemeral keys lost; persistent keys can be explicitly revoked through the Key Management Protocol). [uncertain]
